patches.find: Adding free parameters (elliptical axis ratio, orientation) always reduces residuals and increases R² by definition. The proper test for model improvement requires Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) or Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), which penalize additional parameters. No such comparison is presented. Without AIC/BIC, the claim reduces to overfitting via parameter proliferation.
patches.replace:
Adding free parameters (elliptical axis ratio, orientation) always reduces residuals and increases R² by definition. The proper test for model improvement requires Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) or Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), which penalize additional parameters. No such comparison is presented.

The 48.6% RMS reduction figure cannot be reproduced — no script in the repository computes it. Like the 95.2% accuracy figure (see Section 3.5.6), it appears only in descriptive text with no derivation or verification code. More fundamentally, the RMS improvement is large precisely because the dome is curve-fitting to distances derived from WGS84 globe coordinates. The ellipse is not discovering the shape of a flat disc — it is absorbing the systematic distortion that results from projecting a sphere onto a plane. Any smooth closed curve with enough free parameters will improve the fit to spherically-derived inter-city distances.
patches.rationale: ISS-188/ISS-189: Adds unverifiable 48.6% critique and explains WHY the curve-fit works (absorbing globe projection distortion)

patches.find: Adding parameters always improves fit; no AIC/BIC comparison shown
patches.replace: Adding parameters always improves fit; no AIC/BIC comparison shown; 48.6% RMS figure unverifiable (no computation in repo)
patches.rationale: ISS-021: Summary finding now mentions unverifiable figure

patches.find: The author's own cavity height is H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km. Averaged over the disc, H_eff ≈ 3,400 km. His quarter-wave formula gives f = c/(4 × 3,400) = 22 Hz. The observed Schumann fundamental is 7.83 Hz. His model predicts a frequency 2.8× higher than observed. The stability claim ("Schumann stays at 7.83") is trivially true on the globe: ionospheric conductivity is driven by solar UV, which is stable on decadal timescales. The dome's 22 Hz prediction has never been observed. Claiming credit for the stability of a frequency your model gets wrong by a factor of 3 is not a prediction — it's ignoring the baseline failure. See Section 4.5.1.
patches.replace:
The author's own cavity height is H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km. Depending on the averaging method, H_eff ranges from ~1,800 km (area-weighted) to ~5,200 km (energy-weighted), with a simple radial average of ~3,400 km. The quarter-wave formula f = c/(4H_eff) gives predicted fundamentals of 14–42 Hz — none of which is 7.83 Hz. Even the most favorable averaging gives a frequency 1.8× too high; the simple average gives 2.8× too high. No physically justified averaging of the dome's exponential ceiling produces 7.83 Hz.

The long-term stability of the Schumann fundamental at ~7.83 Hz is well-established in ionospheric physics (Nickolaenko & Hayakawa, 2002). The frequency varies diurnally by ~0.5 Hz and can shift by ~1 Hz during major geomagnetic storms, but the decadal mean remains within ±0.1 Hz. The dome model claims credit for this stability without explaining why a cavity that predicts the wrong frequency would nonetheless hold that wrong frequency constant. See Section 4.5.1.
patches.rationale: ISS-204/ISS-205: Shows full range of averaging methods (all fail), replaces oversimplified stability claim with cited specifics

patches.find: WIN-042 repeats WIN-037 with identical threshold and identical NOAA/INTERMAGNET data sources. The regional SAA decay of 20–35 nT/yr is real but local, while global dipole decay is ~15 nT/yr. Duplicating the same claim across two WIN IDs does not create two independent confirmations.
patches.replace: WIN-037 (PROS-007) claims the global field decayed by ≥28 nT in the 12 months since March 2025. WIN-042 claims the ongoing decay rate is ≥28 nT/year. These are mathematically equivalent: if the field dropped ≥28 nT in any 12-month window, the rate was ≥28 nT/year over that window. Both use the same NOAA World Magnetic Model and INTERMAGNET observatory data. The ≥28 nT/yr threshold is met in SAA stations (regional decay of 20–35 nT/yr) but the global dipole moment is declining at ~5% per century, approximately 15–20 nT/yr in average surface intensity (Thébault et al., 2015, IGRF-12). The dome does not specify whether it means global or regional, allowing it to claim whichever meets the threshold. Duplicating the same claim across two WIN IDs does not create two independent confirmations.
patches.rationale: ISS-217/ISS-219/ISS-220: Shows mathematical equivalence with WIN-037, adds proper citation for decay rate, replaces generic links with specific data portals

patches.find: Hemispheric asymmetry in the Earth's magnetic field is modeled by the CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model, which describes outer core dynamics using spherical harmonic expansions. The asymmetry arises from reversed-flux patches and convection patterns in the outer core. This WIN is part of group WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063 and reuses NOAA data already cited in WIN-004/005.
patches.replace:
Hemispheric asymmetry in the Earth's magnetic field is described by the CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model (Finlay et al., 2020), which uses spherical harmonic decomposition of satellite and observatory data to characterize field evolution. The physical explanation comes from MHD geodynamo simulations (e.g., Aubert et al., 2013, Nature), which derive hemispheric asymmetry from first principles via core-mantle boundary heat flux variations and reversed-flux patches.

The dome model states the asymmetry ratio "matches" without specifying: (a) what numerical ratio is claimed, (b) over what time period, (c) using what definition of "hemispheric" on a flat disc where one hemisphere is the disc center and the other is the rim, or (d) what the dome model actually predicts the ratio to be. Without a specific predicted value derived from dome geometry, any observed ratio automatically "matches." Although the dome's H(r) function is axially symmetric (depending only on radius), the dome describes toroidal aetheric flow with potential directional structure. However, no published formula connects this flow topology to a specific hemispheric decay asymmetry ratio. This WIN is part of group WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063 and reuses NOAA WMM data already cited in WIN-004/005.
patches.rationale: ISS-258/259/260/261: Fixes CHAOS-7 first-principles claim, adds proper paper citations, calls out vagueness of dome claim, acknowledges dome toroidal flow while noting no derivation exists

patches.find: The asymmetry ratio is a consequence of reversed-flux patches in the outer core. The dome model has no core dynamics, no MHD equations, and cannot derive the ratio from its geometry. It observes the ratio and claims it; CHAOS-7 derives it from first principles.
patches.replace: The asymmetry ratio is a consequence of reversed-flux patches in the outer core, derived from first principles via MHD geodynamo simulations (Aubert et al. 2013). The dome model specifies no numerical ratio, no time period, and no derivation — any observed value automatically "matches." CHAOS-7 describes the field; geodynamo theory explains it; the dome merely claims it.
patches.rationale: ISS-259: Corrects attribution (MHD not CHAOS-7 for first principles), emphasizes vagueness

patches.find: The P-wave shadow zone is one of the most powerful pieces of evidence FOR a spherical, layered Earth. Seismic waves refract as they pass through layers of different density. The shadow zone at 104°-140° was first explained by Oldham (1906) and refined by Gutenberg (1913) as proof that Earth has a liquid outer core. The radius and depth of the liquid core (2,891 km depth, outer core from 2,891-5,150 km) are derived directly from this shadow zone geometry on a SPHERE. Claiming this as evidence for a flat disc is self-defeating: the shadow zone calculations assume spherical wave propagation through concentric spherical layers.
patches.replace: The P-wave shadow zone is one of the most powerful pieces of evidence FOR a spherical, layered Earth. Seismic waves refract as they pass through layers of different density. The shadow zone at 104°–140° was first explained by Oldham (1906) and refined by Gutenberg (1913, Physikalische Zeitschrift 14: 1217–1218) as proof that Earth has a liquid outer core. S-waves are completely blocked by the outer core (they cannot propagate through liquid), creating a total S-wave shadow beyond ~104°. P-waves refract around the core and re-emerge as PKP arrivals beyond ~140°, with the shadow zone between these angles. The radius and depth of the liquid core (2,891 km depth, outer core from 2,891–5,150 km) are derived directly from this shadow zone geometry on a SPHERE. On a flat disc 20,015 km in radius, angular distances of 104°–140° (corresponding to 11,600–15,600 km surface distance) have no geometric relationship to a layered spherical refraction pattern. Claiming this as evidence for a flat disc is self-defeating: the shadow zone calculations assume spherical wave propagation through concentric spherical layers.
patches.rationale: ISS-044/045/046: Removes unverified Gutenberg DOI (cites journal instead), standardizes dash style, adds S-wave shadow and PKP arrivals, adds flat-disc geometric impossibility argument
